£18.8M total revenue across all channels. Of 359.4K trackable customers, 63% ordered only once — the biggest growth lever is turning one-timers into regulars.
Growth Stores Menu Customers Predictions Next Steps
Section 01

Growth & Momentum

Is the business growing, and where is the energy?
Key Insight

Till (takeaway) dominates with 50% of revenue. Till (takeaway) grew -23% last complete month — the fastest-moving channel.

Key Insight

Peak demand: Tue at 12:00. Staffing and prep should match this window.

Recommended Actions
  • Align staffing and prep schedules to the demand heatmap.
  • Double down on the fastest-growing channel with targeted promotions.
  • Test discount offers during low-traffic windows to smooth demand.
Section 02

Store Performance

Which outlets are stars and which need attention?
Key Insight

14 outlets analysed across the period. Top 3 stores generate 48% of total revenue. Spitalfields alone accounts for 19%.

Key Insight

AOV spread: Battersea Dark Kitchen (£18.95) vs Spitalfields (£12.95) — a £6.00 gap worth investigating.

Key Insight

Repeat rate across physical stores ranges from 35% (Canary Wharf) to 51% (Bank). (2 dark kitchen(s) excluded — delivery platforms don't share customer identity.)

Recommended Actions
  • Investigate what high-AOV and high-retention stores do differently (menu mix, upselling, service speed, location demographics).
  • Transfer best practices from top performers to lower-ranked stores.
  • Review channel dependency — stores reliant on a single channel are vulnerable to platform changes.
  • Reallocate marketing budget toward highest-ROI locations.
Section 03

Menu Intelligence

What sells, what's profitable, and where are the upsell opportunities?
Key Insight

"Build Your Own Salad" is the top seller, generating 24% of top-15 revenue.

Key Insight

Paid add-ons contribute 39% of customisable bundle revenue. 10% of orders skip add-ons entirely — significant upsell headroom.

Key Insight

"Chipotle caesar dressing" is the most popular dressing — feature it in marketing and default selections.

Recommended Actions
  • Prompt add-on suggestions at checkout for orders with zero paid add-ons — this alone could lift basket size materially.
  • Test bundle pricing to incentivise premium customisation (e.g. 'add avocado for £1').
  • Place high-margin items more prominently on menus and kiosk screens.
Section 04

Customer Base

Who are our customers, and how much are they worth?
Key Insight

63% of customers are one-timers (generating just 24% of revenue), while Power users (1% of base) drive 17% of revenue.

Key Insight

Converting just 10% of one-timers to Light users could add ~£581K in incremental revenue.

Key Insight

Churn varies significantly by store: Bond Street loses 77% of repeat customers, while Battersea Power Station retains best at 54%. High-churn locations may have an experience or service problem worth investigating on-site.

Key Insight

4,679 regular customers (6+ orders) are overdue relative to their own ordering pattern — not yet churned, but silent longer than usual. The 18% who use digital channels can be contacted; the rest can only be flagged if they return in-store.

Recommended Actions
  • For digital-channel customers (18%): automated second-order incentive within a week of their first visit.
  • For till-only customers (82%): prompt account sign-up at the till — this is the only way to enable follow-up contact.
  • Design a loyalty programme rewarding milestones (orders 3, 6, 10) to move Light users toward Regular.
Section 05

Predicting Who Won't Come Back

Which customers are at risk of leaving — and what can we do about it?

What this does

After a customer places their 2nd order, the model looks at everything about their visits so far — how much they spent, how long between visits, what they ordered, which channel they used — and gives them a risk score: how likely are they to never come back?

A customer is labelled “churned” if they placed zero further orders before the end of the data — but only when their silence is long enough to be meaningful (at least 3× their typical gap between visits). Customers who might just be between orders are excluded, so every label is high-confidence. The model ranks customers from highest to lowest risk. The table below shows: if you focus on the riskiest 20%, how many actual leavers do you find?

Delivery platform customers (Deliveroo, UberEats) are excluded — we can’t identify them across visits. All numbers are from a test set of customers the model has never seen before.

Key Insight

After just 2 visits, the model can identify customers likely to disappear. Focusing on the top 5% highest-risk customers finds 1.9× more churners than picking at random — that's where focused, higher-cost actions pay off. The top 20% still delivers 1.5× lift with lighter interventions.

Key Insight

Each customer who stays is worth ~£70 in subsequent orders. Even small improvements in retention translate directly into revenue.

Key Insight

Most customers (82%) are till-only — we can score their risk but cannot email or call them. Any retention action for these customers has to happen in-store, when they're physically present. The 18% who use digital channels (Click & Collect, Kiosk) can be contacted directly.

How well does it work?

After N visits Customers tested % who leave Improvement vs guessing Churners found in top 20% Value of each retained customer
1 45,799 75% 1.2× 24% £54
2 11,163 41% 1.5× 30% £70
3 6,598 28% 1.6× 33% £77
4 4,732 27% 1.9× 38% £86
5 3,531 23% 1.9× 37% £94

Improvement vs guessing: “1.5×” means the model finds 50% more at-risk customers than picking the same number at random. Higher = better. Row highlighted = recommended starting point (after 2 visits).

What to do with the scores
  • Top 5% risk: small group, high confidence — worth focused attention. Personal follow-up for digital-channel customers; extra care at the counter for till-only customers.
  • Top 20% risk: larger group — suits lighter actions. Automated messages for digital-channel customers; loyalty sign-up prompts at the till for till-only customers.
  • Compare churn rates by store to spot locations that lose customers faster than expected — a high churn store may have an experience problem, not a marketing problem.
Section 06

What To Do Next

Concrete actions, ordered from quickest to most ambitious
🔍

Know who's leaving

The churn model scores every returning customer after their 2nd visit. The top 5% highest-risk customers can be flagged for personal attention — the model finds roughly twice as many actual leavers as picking at random. Use this to focus staff effort where it matters most.

Ready now
👤

Convert till-only customers to accounts

82% of trackable customers are till-only — we can score their risk but can't reach them by email or phone. Prompting account sign-up at the till (or via QR on receipts) turns them into contactable customers, unlocking direct follow-up for future retention.

Quick win
📍

Investigate high-churn stores

Churn rates vary significantly between locations. Stores with unusually high churn may have experience problems (slow service, quality inconsistency) rather than a marketing problem. Compare the worst-performing stores against the best to find what's different.

Quick win
🔄

Get the second visit

The biggest drop-off is between order 1 and order 2. A small nudge within the first week — a discount, a thank-you message, a reason to come back — could shift a meaningful number of one-timers into repeat customers. This only works for the 18% on digital channels; for till-only customers, it has to happen in-store.

Quick win
⭐

Build a loyalty programme

Power customers (20+ orders) are a small fraction of the base but drive a disproportionate share of revenue. A programme that rewards milestones (3rd visit, 10th visit, 20th) gives customers a reason to keep choosing you — and gives you a reason to collect their contact details.

Medium-term project
⚡

Real-time risk scoring at the till

When a customer taps their card, the system can look up their history and compute a risk score in milliseconds. Staff can see a simple flag — green / amber / red — and adjust accordingly: a friendly check-in, a complimentary extra, or just making sure the experience is flawless. This is the only way to intervene with the 82% who are till-only.

Requires integration
